content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                                    Date : Jan. 18, 1977

                                                             A-77-01-001

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       /Red trim

Subject: Definition of a Committee.

       Philip Martin, an attorney in San Francisco, asked whether
a legal defense fund for Sheriff Hongisto constitute a com-
mittee within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. I
advised him on January 13, 1977, that the legal defense fund
would be a recipient committee pursuant to Section 82013(a).

2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215 (b)(1) provides that any payment '
a candidate is a contribution unless it is clear from all the
surrounding circumstances that the payment was for personal
purposes unrelated to the person's candidacy or status as an
officeholder.  Initially, we observe that the legal defense
fund is related to Sheriff Hongisto's status as an office-
holder since the legal difficulties involved emanate direct-
ly from his refusal to perform certain duties in his capacity
as sheriff.  Furthermore, we think that Sheriff Hongisto is
a candidate at the present time.  Section 82007 provides that
a person is a candidate whenever he raises or expends funds
or authorizes another to do so on his behalf, for the purpose
of seeking nomination or election to elective office regard-
less of whether the office is known at the present time.  In
the instant case, Sheriff Hongisto has held at least two fund-
raising dinners since his last election to produce previous
campaign debts.  Because it is generally advisable to liqui-
date past campaign debts before seeking future office, the
staff assumes that such fund-raising activities are generally
undertaken with some view of seeking future office, albeit
sometimes remote.

2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215 provides that a candidate re-
ceives a contribution when he receives payment or when a pay-
ment is made at his behest.  Subsection (C) of that regulation
provides that payment is made at the behest of the candidate
when it is made pursuant to his control or direction. In the
instant case, we think payments by the legal defense fund are
made at the behest of Sheriff Hongisto since such funds will
be used to pay the cost of transcripts, fines, and attorney's
fees. Because Sheriff Hongisto is the defendant, we think that

                                                             [41]

Filed
Jan. 19, 1977
p.2


he necessarily exercises control and direction over the defense
case.

Subsection (d) of the regulation provides that a contribution
includes any monetary or non-monetary payment for which full
and adequate consideration is not made to the donor that is
received by any person or organization other than a candidate,
control committee, official arm of a political party, or
primary purpose committee if the payment is earmarked for the
making of contributions or expenditures. A payment is ear-
marked when, at the time of making the payment the donor knows
or has reason to know that the payment or funds with which the
payment will be combined will be used to make contributions.
In the instant case, the donors know that the funds will be
used to assist Sheriff Hongjsto in his legal defense. Since
these payments are unmarked and since the control and direction of card
date Hongjsto in connection with his status as an officeholder
such payments are earmarked as contributions. Since the payments
are contributions, the legal defense fund will be a committee
pursuant to Section 82013(a) if it receives $500 or more in
donations. In addition, a person could become a major donor
pursuant to Section 82013(c) by virtue of such payments to the
legal defense fund.

TP:jo

embedding.qa_text: This memorandum, dated January 18, 1977, is from the Fair Political Practices Commission and addresses the definition of a committee in the context of the Political Reform Act. Philip Martin, an attorney in San Francisco, asked whether a legal defense fund for Sheriff Hongisto constitutes a committee within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. Ted Prim advised him on January 13, 1977, that the legal defense fund would be a recipient committee pursuant to Section 82013(a). Section 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215 (b)(1) provides that any payment a candidate receives is a contribution unless it is clear from all the surrounding circumstances that the payment was for personal purposes unrelated to the person's candidacy or status as an officeholder. Initially, we observe that the legal defense fund is related to Sheriff Hongisto's status as an officeholder since the legal difficulties involved emanate directly from his refusal to perform certain duties in his capacity as sheriff. Furthermore, we think that Sheriff Hongisto is a candidate at the present time. Section 82007 provides that a person is a candidate whenever he raises or expends funds, or authorizes another to do so on his behalf, for the purpose of seeking nomination or election to elective office regardless of whether the office is known at the present time. In the instant case, Sheriff Hongisto has held at least two fundraising dinners since his last election to reduce previous campaign debts. Because it is generally advisable to liquidate past campaign debts before seeking future office, the staff assumes that such fund-raising activities are generally undertaken with some view of seeking future office, albeit sometimes remote. Section 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215 provides that a candidate receives a contribution when he receives payment or when a payment is made at his behest. Subsection (c) of that regulation provides that payment is made at the behest of the candidate when it is made pursuant to his control or direction. In the instant case, we think payments by the legal defense fund are made at the behest of Sheriff Hongisto since such funds will be used to pay the cost of transcripts, fines, and attorney's fees. Because Sheriff Hongisto is the defendant, we think that he necessarily exercises control and direction over the defense case. Subsection (d) of the regulation provides that a contribution includes any monetary or non-monetary payment for which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor that is received by any person or organization other than a candidate, control committee, official arm of a political party, or primary purpose committee if the payment is earmarked for the making of contributions or expenditures. A payment is earmarked when at the time of making the payment the donor knows or has reason to know that the payment or funds with which the payment will be commingled will be used to make contributions.
